text: CASTRO-CENIZA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 24 m. SSE of Burgos, on the l. bank of the Mataviejas.

CASTRO-D'AVELÆS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the r. bank of the Amazon, near the confluence of the Ica or Putumajo. Its inhabitants are Indians of the Cambevas tribe, and subsist chiefly on the produce of the chase and fishing. The culture of the soil is performed by the women.
CASTRO-D'AYRO, a town of Portugal, in the chilla. Pop. 930. It contains several churches, a CAS Pop. 2,500. It contains a church and an hospital.
CASTRO-DE-CALDELAS, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 29 m. ENE of Orense, partido of Puebla-de-Tribes, on the l. bank of the Castro.
CASTRO-DE-LA-LOMA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NW of Leon, partido of Muriasde-Paredes, on the r. bank of the Orvigo.

CASTRO-DEL-RIO-EL-LEAL, a town of Spain, in Andalucia, in the prov. and 20 m. SE of Cordova, partido of Baena, on the r. bank of the Guadajocello. Pop. 9,092. It contains several churches, a customhouse, a public school, a convent, &c.; and has some manufactories of woollen fabrics and linen.

CASTRO-DE-URDIALES, a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 27 m. E of Santander, on the bay of Biscay. Pop. 2,930. It is of considerable antiquity; and contains an old castle in a good state of preservation, a church, a custom-house, a convent, an hospital, and a school. The port is small, and not well-sheltered.
CASTRODEZA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 11 m. W of Valladolid, partido of Motadel-Marqués.

CASTRO-FUERTE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 26 m. S of Leon, partido and 5 m. S of Valencia-de-Don-Juan, on a hill, near the l. bank of the Esla. Pop. 590. It contains several churches, and a custom-house.

CASTRO-GIOVANNI, a circondario and town of Sicily, in the prov. of Caltanisetta, district and 17 m. NNW of Piazza, on a plateau, at an alt. of 3,280 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 12,000. It contains several churches and convents, and a college. A fair is held once a-year. The environs are fertile, and contain beds of bituminous schist, coloured clay, gypsum, iron pyrites, and sulphureous springs. C. occupies the site of the ancient Enna, celebrated as the most venerated of the sanctuaries of Ceres; and in the vicinity is the lake of Pergusa.

CASTRO-GONZALO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 39 m. N of Zamora, partido and 4 m. E of Benavente, on the l. bank of the Esla, which is here crossed by a bridge. Pop. 803. It contains a church, and a custom-house.

CASTRO-JERIZ, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 21 m. W of Burgos, on a rising ground between the Orda and Garbanzuela, which are here crossed by stone bridges. Pop. 2,434.—This town is of remote antiquity. The remains of a castle still exist on an adjacent hill, supposed to be those of the Castrum Cæsaris.
CASTRO-JIMENO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 29 m. NNE of Segovia, partido and 9 m. NW of Sepulveda, near the l. bank of the Duraton.

CASTRO-LABOREIRO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, comarca and 40 m. NE of Barcellos, and 35 m. NNE of Braga. Pop. 1,600. It is built on an elevated plateau, and is considered one of the coldest localities in Portugal.
CASTRO-MEMBIBRE, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. of Valladolid, partido of Mota-del-Marqués.

CASTRO-MIRIM, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, comarca and 15 m. ENE of Tavira, on the r. bank of the Guadiana, near its mouth, and nearly opposite Ayamonte. Pop. 2,500, chiefly fishermen. Its fortifications are much dilapidated, and it is ill-built. It contains a church and an hospital. In the environs are extensive salt springs.

CASTRO-MOCHO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 14 m. W of Palencia, partido of Freconvent, and a custom-house; and has some manufactories of common cloth, and a considerable trade in cattle.
CASTRO-MONTE, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 16 m. WNW of Valladolid, partido and 7 m. S of Rioseco, on the r. bank of the Badajoz.
CASTRO-NOVO, a fortified town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 38 m. SSE of Palermo, district and 25 m. S of Termini. Pop. 5,800. Marble is wrought in the environs.

CASTRO-NUEVO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 6 m. NE of Valladolid, partido of Villalon, near the r. bank of the Esgueva.—Also a town in the prov. and 20 m. NNE of Zamora, partido of Toro, on the r. bank of the Valderaduey.

CASTRO-NUNO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 33 m. SW of Valladolid, partido of Nava-del-Rey, on a hill near the l. bank of the Duero. Pop. 1,865. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a church, a convent, and a custom-house. Cattle and fish form the chief commerce of the locality.
CASTRO-NUOVO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, district and 26 m. ENE of Lago Negro, and 40 m. SSE of Potenza. Pop. 2,160. A fair is held here once a-year.

CASTRO-PIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise or Sannio, district and 5 m. WNW of Campobasso, near the l. bank of the Biferno. Pop. 2,758. It contains 4 churches, a convent, and a paper-mill.
CASTRO-PODAME, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, partido and 5 m. ENE of Pontferrada, between the Bocza and Tablateglio.

CASTROPOL (SANTIAGO DE), a town and port of Spain, in Asturias, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 60 m. WNW of Oviedo, near the entrance of the Eo or Miranda into the bay of Biscay. Pop. 1,575. It contains a church, several chapels, a custom-house, and an old fortress; and has manufactories of sack-cloth and paper. The partido comprises 36 parishes.
CASTROPONCE, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 44 m. NW of Valladolid, partido of Valoria-la-Buena, on the l. bank of the Valderaduey.

CASTROREALE, a town of Sicily, cap. of a district of the same name, in the valley and 27 m. WSW of Messina, on the Castro. Pop. 3,462. The environs produce wine and oil, and contain a thermal ferruginous spring. The district comprises 5 cant.
CASTROSERNA-DE-ABAJO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 24 m. NE of Segovia, partido and 8 m. SSE of Sepulveda, on the l. bank of the Pradena.
CASTROSERNA-DE-ARRIBA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 24 m. ENE of Segovia, partido and 9 m. SSE of Sepulveda, on the 1. bank of the Pradena.
CASTROVEGA-Y-VEGUELINA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 23 m. SE of Leon, partido of Valencia-de-Don-Juan.
CASTROVERDE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, comarca and 7 m. NE of Ourique, on the l. bank of the Corbes. Pop. 2,300. It contains a collegiate church, an hospital, and an almshouse.

CASTROVERDE-DE-CAMPOS, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 42 m. NNE of Zamora, partido of Benavente, between the Cea and Valderabuey, the latter of which is here crossed by a stone bridge. Pop. 998. It contains 3 churches, a convent, a custom-house, and a castle, now in ruins.
CASTROVERDE-DE-CERRATO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 32 m. ENE of Valladolid, partido of Villalon, on the r. bank of the Esgueva. 374 CAT
CASTRO-VICENTE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 30 m. NNE of Torre-de-Moncorvo, near the r. bank of the Azilo.
CASTRO-VIEGO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov., partido and 11 m. SW of Logrono, on the r. bank of the Yalde.

CASTRO-VILLARE, a fortified town of Naples, cap. of a district of the same name, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, on the l. bank of the Coscilello, 39 m. N of Cosenza. Pop. 7,100. It is well-built, and contains 3 churches, several convents, and an hospital. Its commerce consists chiefly in articles of local produce, the principal of which are wine, fruit, cotton, manna, and silk.—The district comprises 10 cantons.

CASTROVIREINA, a province and town of Peru, in the dep. of Ayacucho. The prov. is bounded on the N by that of Angeraez; on the E by the prov. of Cangallo; on the S by that of Lucanas; and on the W by Lima; comprising a superficies of 66 m. in length from E to W, and about 75 m. in breadth. Pop. 7,000. It is generally mountainous and unproductive, and the climate ungenial. In the valleys, however, wheat, maize, fruit, and potatoes are successfully cultivated, and they afford good pasturage to numerous herds of cattle and sheep. The town occupies an elevated site near an affluent of the Mantaro.

CASTUA, a town of Illyria, in the gov. and 35 m. SW of Trieste, circle of Pisino, 6 m. NW of Fiume, on a rocky eminence near the shore of the gulf of Quarnero. Pop. 450. It possesses some commerce in wine, oil, and fruit, but is now much decayed. It was the Liburnia of the ancients.

CASTUERA, a judicial partido and town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 69 m. ESE of Badajoz. The partido comprises 12 towns. The town is situated at the extremity of the Sierra-de-Cabezade-Buey, near the Guadalefra. Pop. 6,572. It contains a church, and a custom-house.
CASTULAS, a town of Spain, in Andalucia, in the prov. and 31 m. S of Granada, partido of Motril.
CASUARINA (POINT), a headland of Western Australia, in the district of Wellington, at the entrance of Leschenault inlet, in S lat. 33° 18'.

CASWELL, a county of the state of North Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 400 sq. m., drained by Dan river and its branches. Its soil is generally fertile. Pop. in 1830, 15,188; in 1840, 14,693; of whom 7,343 were whites, 7,024 slaves, and 326 free coloured. The cap. is Ganceyville.
CAT ISLAND, or SAN SALVADOR, originally Gu-

ANAHANI, one of the Bahama islands, having its SE extremity in N lat. 24° 8', W long. 75° 13'. Its length from SE to NW is 46 m.; its general width, about 3 m. It was the first American land seen by Columbus on 12th October, 1492; and by him was named San Salvador. There are two convict settlements upon this island: one containing, in 1847, 348 males; the other, 326 females. The amount of waste land upon it in 1847 was 28,785 acres.—Also an island in the gulf of Mexico, 52 m. NE by E of New Orleans, in N lat. 30° 15'. It is about 6 m. in length, and a quarter of a mile in breadth.—Also an island in the Mergui archipelago, off the coast of Siam, in N lat. 10° 20'. It is about 4 m. in circumf.
CAT KAYS, a group of islets on the Great Bahama bank, in N lat. 25° 323', W long. 79° 15'. They are distinguished as the Dog, Wolf, and Cat kays. On the Dog kay there is a lighthouse.
CAT LAKE. See CHAT (LAC-DU).
CATABAWS, an aboriginal tribe of N America, who were located on the banks of the river of the same name, on the confines of the Carolinas, U.S.
CATAHAS, an Indian tribe of North America, located on the W bank of the Missouri, between the Big Horn and the Platte.

CATAHOOLA, a parish in the state of Louisiana, U. S, comprising an area of 2,100 sq. m.; bounded on the E by the Tensas river, and on the SE by a lake of the same name, and intersected by Wachita river, and by Macon and Bouff bayous. The W part is hilly, and covered to a great extent with pine timber. The E is low, and liable to inundation, but generally fertile. Pop. in 1830, 2,581; in 1840, 4,955, of whom 2,935 were whites, 1,998 slaves, and 22 free coloured. Cap., Harrisonburg.—Also a river which takes its rise in Claiborne parish; runs SE; and falls into the Wachita river, after spreading into a lake-like expanse which in autumn becomes nearly dry, and forms a prairie.
CATALANO, a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 3 m. SW of Syracuse.
CATALAO, a small island of Brazil, in the bay of Nitherohi, near Caju point, and about 4 m. NE of Rio-de-Janeiro.

CATALDO (SAN), a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the district and 4 m. WSW of Caltanicetta. Pop. 7,897. In the environs are sulphurworks, the annual produce of which amounts to 24,000 cantars.
CATALINA, or CUSUBATI, a river of Peru, which descends from the E branch of the Andes; runs E, and passes through a lake of the same name; and falls into the Paro in S lat. 6° 7'.
CATALINA HARBOUR, a port of Newfoundland, on the E coast, to the N of Trinity bay, and 12 m. S of Cape Bonavista, in N lat. 48° 35'.
CATALINA (LA), a promontory of the island of St. Domingo, on the S coast, in W long. 70° 30'.
CATALINA (SANTA), a village and small fort of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 29 m. WSW of Coimbra, at the embouchure of the Mondego.

CATALINA (SANTA), a town of Venezuela, in the dep. of Apure, and 60 m. SW of Varinas .- Also a town of La Plata, in the prov. and 50 m. NNW of Cordova.—Also a fort in the same prov., 130 m. S of Cordova.—Also a town in the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the NW coast near Point Dille. - Also an island near the SE coast of St. Domingo, in N lat. 18° 15', and W long. 68° 58'.—Also an island about 45 m. in circumference, near the entrance of San Pedro bay, Upper California, in N lat. 33° 20'.—Also a small island of the Caribbean sea, to the N of, and separated by a very narrow channel from, Old Providence, in N lat. 13° 20', and W long. 80° 40'. It is a craggy uninhabited rock.
CATALINA (SANTA), or CERRALBO, an island of the gulf of California, to the N of Cape Arenas, in N lat. 24° 10′ and W long. 109° 40′.
CATALINA (PUNTA SANTA), a headland of Guatimala, at the S side of the gulf of Papagayo, in N lat. 10° 30′, and W long. 85° 40′.
CATALLA, a district of Lower Guinea, in Benguela, near the Bumbo territory, and 180 m. S of S. Philip de Benguela.

CATALONI'A, or CATALUNA, a province of Spain, forming the NE extremity of the peninsula. It lies between the parallels of 40° 30′ and 42° 40′; and the meridians of 0° 15' and 3° 15' E long.; and is bounded on the N by the Pyrenees, which separate it from France; on the E and S by the Mediterranean; on the SW by Valencia; and by Aragon on the W. It extends from NE to SW about 170 m., and 130 from NW to SE. Its area is estimated at 12,606 sq. m. by Hassel. In the Almanach de Gotha its superficies is stated at 573.2 German, or 12,173 English sq. m. This prov. is almost throughout extremely mountainous, but is interspersed with rich and well-cultivated plains and valleys, which are watered and fertilized by numerous rivers. The coasts are bold and rugged. Mountains.] The Pyrenees here run along the French boundaries, and spread their branches through the whole prov. On the S of the prov. a range of mountains called Montsia enters C. from Valencia, and running to the NE, strikes the Ebro, from the l. bank of which river, the chain is continued, in the Sierra-de-la-Llona, and other mountains, which form a connected range of highlands intersecting the centre of the prov., and stretching to its N frontier. This range divides the basin of the Segre on the W from that of the Llobregat on the E. In the Molacima, the Montsia attains an alt. of 2,504 ft. [Mechain]; or 2,740 ft. [Antillon]. - The principal river is the Ebro, which intersects the S part of the prov.; and, passing Tortosa, and Amposta, enters the Mediterranean by 3 deltoid branches. At the point where the Ebro enters C. from Aragon, it receives on the l. bank the Segre, which rises in the Pyrenees near Andorra, and flowing S, is joined by the Noguera Palleresa near Balaguer, and the Noguera-de-Ribagorza a little above its junction with the Ebro. The Llobregat rises near Baga, and flowing S, receives the Cardener near Manresa. The Ter, the Fluvia, and the Muga, water the NE part of C. - Near the centre of the prov. rises Montserrat, or Monserrate [alt. 4,053 ft.], remarkable both for situation and appearance, and the composition and arrangement of its rocks. It appears to stand completely insulated and unconnected with any other mountain, rearing its bare and rugged head above a hilly country, like a pile of Gothic spires. Its huge cliffs shoot up to a stupendous elevation; and their blanched and naked summits are split into a variety of shapes. Some of these rocks are composed of limestone of different colours, cemented together by sand and a yellow calcareous earth; others, of freestone and white quartz, mixed with touchstone. Indeed, the whole of Montserrat is formed of that species of aggregation, known to naturalists by the name of pudding- Though most of the mountain appears stone. blanched and barren, yet the interstices among the cliffs present luxuriant vegetation. These spaces are filled with close woods and deciduous trees, intermingled with aromatic shrubs and flowers, which are nourished by the rich earth which is washed down from the rocks by the rains. Between Figueras and Gerona, are two mountains standing together, and remarkable for their uniformity. They are both of an equal height, and of a pyramidal form; and, according to Bowles, have all the signs of ancient volcanoes. One of the greatest natural curiosities of this prov. is the mountain of salt in the vicinity of Cardona. See Cardona. Near Olot, about 55 m. N of Barcelona, is a district of extinct volcanoes. -The other mountains of C. contain valuable minerals. Marble is found in great variety, and wrought to a considerable extent. There are several iron mines near Alins and Taull; lead-mines exist near Tortosa; of numerous coal-mines, that of Clansa is the best. Alum is abundant in the valley of Arun, and nitre in the plains of Urgel. Amethysts, topazes, and coloured crystals, are found near Vicq, and are manufactured and sold by the goldsmiths of Barcelona; there are also mines of antimony, copper, tin, vitriol, silver, and one of gold.—Among the mineral waters, the most remarkable cold springs are those of Monistrol, Vall-de-Ebron, Tortosa, and Rivas; the principal hot springs are at Caldas, Garriga, Gerona, Espluga, and Torello. Climate.] In the highlands of C., the heat and cold are equally severe in their respective seasons; in the valleys, the summer and winter are temperate. In the interior of the country the air is dry, and near the coast it is damp. Soil and agriculture.] Though the soil of this prov. | is more ungrateful, and more interrupted with mountains and rocks than that of any other district of Spain, yet about half the surface is susceptible of cultivation; and agriculture is here better understood, and more successfully and universally practised, than in any other part of Spain. The plains and valleys are intersected by numerous canals for the purposes is established for conducting and distributing the gating his land for a limited time, according to the an annual duty, which is expended in keeping up plains of Urgel, Vallez, and Iqualada; the environs of Cervera, the Campo-de-Tarragona, and Ampurdan. The industry of the Catalonians, however, is not confined to their plains. The mountains are improved by their skill and labour; and even craggy cliffs, which seem to have been destined for ever to be the habitation of the chamois, are converted by their industry into vineyards, cultivated fields, and valuable forests. In many places they carry up earth to fix the young vine in; and rather than suffer a good patch of soil to remain useless, they will let one another down to it from the brow of the rock by ropes. Planting is a favourite object of attention with the Catalonian landlords. Plantations of beeches, elms, poplars, pines, and oaks, are consequently numerous and thriving; and cork-trees are so plentiful, that C. is able to supply the whole of Europe with this important article. Fruit trees of every description are reared in great abundance, particularly in the beautiful gardens on the banks of the Segra, the Llobregat, and the Ampurdan, and between Mataro and Barcelona; and oranges, lemons, figs, almonds, filberts, apples, walnuts, and chestnuts, are abundant throughout the province. Grain of every kind is raised; but the crops are liable to mildew, and the wheat-harvest is seldom sufficient for the consumption of the inhabitants, who are obliged to draw supplies from other countries. The cultivation of rice has of late considerably decreased, from the idea that it produces an effect upon the atmosphere very prejudicial to the health. The people of Ampurdan, who were its first victims, in a moment of irritation destroyed most of their rice fields. The vintage in C. is, in general, uncommonly plentiful, and the wine of the E part of the province is excellent; that of the W and N parts is rather of an inferior quality. The best red wine is made at Mataro, and the best white at Sitges. C. produces annually upwards of 600,000 pipes; from this quantity are made about 30,000 pipes of brandy, the rest is shipped-off or consumed in the country. The Spaniards are excelled by their French neighbours in the distillation of wines into brandy, as much as in the making of wines. Spanish brandies are often foul, acrid, and taste of aniseed, which is liked in Spain and cannot be eradicated from their stills and casks. The exports are made from Barcelona, Salon, Tarragona and Mataro. The red wines are very coarse, being carelessly made and hastily shipped-off. These are the government contract-wines, well-known to the sailors of the British navy as 'black strap.' The white wines of C. are less exceptionable. The Malmsey of Sitges is highly spoken of. Olive trees are in great abundance; but mulberries, though they thrive well, are neglected, consequently less silk is produced here than in many of the other provinces. The cultivation of flax and hemp is also inconsiderable; and the wool is altogether insufficient to supply the manufactories of the provinces. The principal productions of C., with their average annual quantity and price in 1786 are exhibited in the following table: Quantity. Value. £ s. d. Wheat, The roads in this prov. are in general bad; and, notwithstanding the industry of its inhabitants in other respects, they are almost totally neglected. There are few bridges; and in rainy weather the traveller is liable to be constantly stopped by the rivers, which in that season descend in torrents from the mountains. Goods are conveyed in carts drawn by four or five mules yoked in a line, which carry immense weights; and the Catalans are reckoned the most expert and careful drivers in Spain. The great coast-road, after leaving Barcelona, divides into two branches, one of which, passing to Gerona, runs N, crosses the Pyrenees at Bellegarde, and proceeds to Perpignon; the other pursues a more direct N course to Ripoll, and, crossing the Pyrenees, descends into France by the valley of the Tech. Another great line of road passes from Lerida up the valley of the Segre, and crosses the Pyrenees at Port Seguier. Manufactures and commerce.] C. from remote times has been famous for its manufactures; and here every profession connected with the useful arts is as much honoured and respected as in the other provinces of Spain it is despised. Its woollen and cotton stuffs, silks, velvets, and linens, were celebrated even in the 13th cent.; and though its manufactures and commerce afterwards met with several severe checks, and were almost annihilated in the 16th and the beginning of the 18th cent., yet from that time their revival has been gradual and constant; and C. is now the most active and industrious province in the kingdom. Besides cloths of almost every description, C. has manufactures of stockings, laces, tapes, nets, ribbons, &c.; also of iron ware, cutlery, earthen ware, and shoes, for exportation; and above 200 mills are employed in making paper, which produce annually about 480,000 reams, estimated at £160,000 sterling.—These articles, together with its natural productions, supply C. with many valuable exports; and its great extent of coast and excellent harbours render it peculiarly favourable for an extensive commerce. Of its coarse cloths and serges, the greater part is made up into clothing for the troops, and sent to the different provinces of Spain; the fine cloths are carried to Madrid, Aragon, &c. Two-thirds of its linens, cotton-velvets, nankeens, and muslins used to be exported to the colonies; about one-twelfth and a half was kept for the consumption of the prov.; and the remaining twotwelfths and a half were sold in the markets of the peninsula. About 10,000 dozen of silk-handkerchiefs and sashes, manufactured at Manresa, were annually sent to Aragon, Biscay, and the two Castiles, and 35,000 to America; 30,000 quintals of cork, and 470,000 reams of paper, were exported to different parts of Europe; England received 20,000 sacks of nuts and 10,000 pipes of brandy from C.; 4,000 pipes of brandy were exported to Guernsey and Alderney, and about 20,000 to Holland and the north of Europe; 8,000 loads of oil were carried to France and Holland, and 4,000 loads of wine to Italy; 200,000 pairs of shoes were exported to America, and 500,000 distributed in the interior of the kingdom; and of 24,000 pairs of worsted stockings furnished by the town of Vicq, one-half passed into the adjacent provinces. This prov. is the principal seat of the cotton manufacture in Spain. From the statistical statements published by the Spanish government, it would appear that in the year 1839 there were in C. 18,204 looms, giving employment to 102,213 workmen, whose annual wages might amount to 190,000,000 reals (19 reals equal to 5 francs), and who spun and manufactured 11,000,000 lbs. of cotton wool into ordinary and common goods, whilst in the Balearic islands there were 2,000 looms. The total capital employed in this trade throughout Spain was estimated at 25,000,000 piastres; and the goods produced in each year were valued at 521,556,305 reals. The imports of this prov. consist chiefly of corn, wool, and silk. From 400,000 to 600,000 quarters of wheat are supposed to be annually imported from America, Sicily, and the N of Europe; and, in 1775, Canada alone sent about 80,000 quarters. It receives 10,000 quintals of wool and 80,000 lbs. of silk from Aragon; 100,000 lbs. of silk from Valencia; and Lyons silks, Gange and Nismes silkstockings, fine cloths, linens, essences, perfumes, pomatums, jewellery, and millinery, from France. England furnishes it with superfine cottons, herrings, and cod - fish; and Holland with a few spices. The amount of these, however, is far from being equal to its imports. About 1,000 vessels are almost constantly employed in trade; and the government can depend upon 18,000 Catalonian seamen, who are registered, and always ready for any emergency. Population.] The pop. of C. since the beginning of the last cent. has been upon the increase. In 1718, it amounted to 407,132; in 1768, to 722,506; in 1788, to 814,412. In this last enumeration were 6,608 priests, 5,801 monks and nuns, 1,266 nobles, 6,968 students, 1,020 writers and lawyers, and 20,963 domestics. In 1799 it was returned at 858,818; and in 1834, at 1,041,222. The spirit of industry which pervades C. has led its inhabitants to turn their exertions to the useful rather than to the liberal arts; and, as mechanics, they are in general preferred to those of any other province. The sciences, however, have not been entirely neglected; and among the Catalans have arisen several writers upon natural history, theology, medicine, and law, of no inconsiderable merit. Besides public libraries, and schools of different kinds, there were formerly two academies in this province: one at Barcelona, founded in the end of the 14th cent. by John I., king of Aragon; and the other at Tortosa, founded in the beginning of the 15th, where verses were recited and prizes decreed to the victors. The existence of both, however, was but short; and since their declension, the Catalans have made little progress in the fine arts.-In their dispositions the Catalans seem to be influenced by the nature of their country. They are a hardy, impetuous race, of a brown complexion, strong features, and brawny limbs; their chief characteristics are activity and a spirit of independence. Their enterprising spirit and love of money lead them to every quarter of the world; and there are few sea-ports in Europe, India, or Spanish America, where Catalans are not to be found. As soldiers, their bravery and firmness has been always conspicuous, and has been often displayed against the most fearful odds. In the beginning of the 18th cent. they withstood the united armies of France and Spain; and in 1714 the inhabitants of Barcelona made one of the most vigorous and desperate defences that is recorded in history. On a forlorn hope, or for a coup-de-main, the Catalans are excellent; but they are in general averse to the strictness of regular discipline. When treated with kindness and mild words, they are docile and obedient; but they rise against the slightest appearance of authority or subordination. The Catalans possess a haughtiness of demeanour, and authoritative tone, peculiar to themselves. But though high-spirited and overbearing, they are distinguished for their honesty, steadiness, and sobriety. Most of the principal families in Madrid have Catalans at the head of their affairs; and as muleteers and callesieros, they are scattered over every prov. of the peninsula. Instead of the mantle and jacket common throughout Spain, the usual dress, in almost all ranks among the Catalans, is a close coat in the French fashion; the peasants on the mountains wear a double-breasted vest, with a kind of wide greatcoat called a gambeto, which reaches only to the knee. Their ancient and favourite slouched hats, white shoes, and large brown cloaks, were long ago prohibited.-C. has a language distinct from the other provinces of Spain. It is the ancient Limousine tongue, a dialect of the Romance or Provencal, but disfigured by hard terminations, and by a sharp and disagreeable pronunciation, which gives an appearance of greater roughness and asperity to the Catalonian character than in reality belongs to it. This language is spoken throughout the prov., but with considerable variations in the different districts, and is preferred to the Castilian. It is purest on the mountains, but is greatly mixed with Castilian in the large towns. Its harshness, however, is completely lost in the mouth of a Valencian, whose pronunciation renders it soft and harmonious; and though in the towns of Valencia the Castilian is always used, yet the Limousine in some parts of that province has preserved its ancient purity. Divisions, &c.] This prov. forms one of the military divisions of the kingdom under the gov. of a captain-general. It is subdivided into the 4 administrative provinces of Barcelona, Tarragona, Gerona, and Lerida. Its principal towns are Barcelona the capital, Tarragona, Urgel, Lerida, Gerona, Salsona, Vicq, Tortosa, Figueras, Aulot, Igualda, Reus, Mataro, Villa-Franca-de-Panades, Cervera, Manresa, and Palamos. History.] C. with the rest of Spain, was overwhelmed by the irruptions of the northern barbarians in the beginning of the 5th cent., and continued under the dominion of the Goths until 712, when it was compelled to yield to the Saracens. The Saracen power in this province, however, was not of long continuance; for before the conclusion of the same cent., they were completely expelled by Louis le Debonaire, the son of Charlemagne. It was then erected into a prov. of France, and was governed by counts or viceroys, removeable at pleasure, until Godfrey or Wifred the Hairy was created hereditary count of Barcelona, and the sovereignty of the prov. bestowed upon him and his heirs for ever. As soon as it became a separate power, C. rose to respectability among the independent nations of Europe; and besides Catalonia, the counts of Barcelona comprised under their dominion Roussillon, Cerdagne, the country of Foix, and a great part of Languedoc. At this time C. was divided into vigueries or jurisdictions, each governed by a viguier or lieutenant, who enjoyed a very considerable authority; and when its count, Raimond V., succeeded to the crown of Aragon in 1137, by his marriage with Petronilla, the heiress of that kingdom, C. still continued to be governed by its own states, which shared in the legislative power with the sovereign. These states were composed of the three orders, the clergy, the nobility, and the commons, whose power consisted in proposing to the king such laws as they thought necessary, and in sanctioning those that originated with him. This privilege was continued even after its union with the crown of Spain; but it was gradually undermined by the viceroys, and at last completely withdrawn by Philip V., who allowed them to retain merely the empty right of sending deputies to the states general of the Spanish monarchy. C., during its union with Aragon, was engaged in repeated, and sometimes lengthened rebellions. It frequently opposed the most obstinate resistance to the commands of its sovereign, and even often attempted to choose a foreign prince as its ruler, or to erect itself into a republic. But notwithstanding its exertions for independence, it continued under the dominion of Aragon, and afterwards of Spain, till 1640, when the Catalans, enraged at the arrest of their deputies at Madrid by the command of Philip IV., flew to arms, and declared themselves free. Being unable, however, long to maintain their freedom, they gave themselves up to France, and in 1641 proclaimed Louis count of Barcelona. But even the power of Louis was insufficient to support them against Spain. They were reduced in 1652 by Don John of Austria, and in 1659 their submission was confirmed by the treaty of the Pyrenees. The most desperate and obstinate struggle, however, which the Catalans made in defence of their privileges, was in the war of the succession, when attaching themselves to the cause of the Archduke Charles, they resisted every offer of accommodation from the French; and when at last deserted by the king whom they had chosen, and by every foreign power, they stood alone, they yet maintained the contest against the united efforts of Spain and France; but by this war the prov. was drained of its wealth, and almost depopulated, as many of its inhabitants, preferring exile to submission, emigrated to foreign countries. Its manufactures and commerce were destroyed; and when Barcelona capitulated in 1714, Philip V. received a country without resources; every privilege was abolished, and heavy taxes imposed in order to defray the expenses of its reduction. But though depressed and impoverished, it in a short time recovered its activity and energy; and its trade being relieved from the impolitic and paralyzing taxes of alcavala cientos and millones, under which many of the other provs. of the Spanish monarchy labour, it soon rose to its present state of wealth and splendour. Except a few partial insurrections, the Catalans have submitted to the yoke with sullen apathy; and such is their character of pride and independence, that several of their nobility had constantly refused to accept of any titles and dignities, till the last journey of Charles IV. into this province, who, by his kindness and manners, greatly conciliated its inhabitants.
CATAMARCA, or San Fernando-de-Cata-

MARCA, a town of La Plata, the cap. of the prov. of the same name, in a beautiful and well-watered valley, enclosed on the E by the Sierra Ancasta, 130 m. WSW of Tucuman, and 200 m. SSW of Salta. It possesses little trade, but the surrounding district is extremely fertile, producing corn, wine, red pepper, cotton of excellent quality, and abundance of pasturage.—The prov. of C. has a pop. of about 30,000; of whom about 4,000 are in the capital, and the greater portion of the remainder are located in the valley of C., which runs from NW to SE, from the confines of Atacama to those of Rioja. CATAMAYU, CHIRA, or COLAN, a river which takes its rise on the E side of the Savanilla, a summit of the Andes, in the prov. of Loxa, in New Grenada, runs N to the confluence of the Gonzanama; thence bends W; enters Peru; and crossing the district of Piura, flows, after a total course of 198 m., into the Pacific at Colan, between Capes Parina and Payta.
CATAMBUCO, a town of New Grenada, in Ecuador, 5 m. SE of Pasto, and 130 m. NE of Quito, on the road from that town to Popayan.
CATANA, a town of Tunis, on the gulf and 21 m. SE of Cabes.

CATANDUANES, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Philippine group, near the SE extremity of the island of Luzon, in N lat. 13° 47', E long. 124° 10'. It is 36 m. in length from N to S, and averages 19 m. in breadth. The soil is fertile, and is watered by a river of the same name, and several minor streams. It produces palm-oil, cacao, rice, honey, and wax; and gold is found in the gravel of the rivers. The inhabitants are industrious, and employ themselves in agriculture, fishing, and the construction of a light species of boat which they sell to the adjacent islanders. The women wear a long loose covering, over a species of tunic, and adorn themselves with rings and other ornaments of gold.
CATANHEDE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 15 m. WNW of Coimbra. Pop. 1,000. It contains a church, a convent, an hospital, and an alms-house.

CATA'NIA, the ancient Catina, a town of Sicily, in CAT Mount Ætna, in the Val-di-Noto, on the shore of the Ionian sea, and 31 m. NNW of Syracuse. Situated upon a declivity and the plain at its base, the aspect of C. is magnificent and imposing, from the number of public buildings, the width and regularity of the streets, and the size of many of the houses. It contains upwards of 50 religious edifices of different descriptions, including churches, convents, and monasteries. These buildings are constructed of lava, and faced with magnesian limestone from Malta or Syracuse. The cathedral, dedicated to St. Agatha, was founded in 1094, and richly endowed by Roger the Norman; but it has often suffered dilapidation from earthquakes, and its appearance is said to be greatly altered from the repairs it has received. The Benedictine convent of San Nicolo, which occupies a large space in the higher part of the town, is a spacious mass of building capable of accommodating more than twice the number of its inmates. The size and splendour of the convent bespeak the opulence of the order. Nobility of birth is an indispensable requisite, exacted by the rule of the convent from those who are candidates for a share of its comforts. After Mafra in Portugal, this is the finest monastic building in Europe. In the church of the convent there is an exquisite organ, inferior only to the celebrated instrument in Antwerp. The museum, occupying one wing of the convent, contains many valuable curiosities, the most interesting of which have been found in Sicily and Italy. Narrow streets with lofty houses have often been reprobated as an error in the construction of towns in warm climates; the modern C., however, shows that the greatest possible inconvenience results from the streets being very wide, and the houses low. They are so spacious that the buildings scarcely afford any shade, consequently the heat and glare of light produced by the direct rays of the sun, together with the reflection from the lava, of which both the street and buildings are formed, are intolerable. The palaces of the nobility have been begun upon so magnificent a scale, that few of them are finished. These palaces are built in the form of a square, with one side to the street; the first upper floors are usually occupied by the proprietor. The lower part forms a striking contrast to an English eye. Here cobblers, and all sorts of mechanics of the lowest orders, are to be seen at work in their cells when driven in by the heat, or on the pavement when the coolness allows them to breathe a purer air. The city is superabundantly stocked with priests, monks, nuns, and beggars. Any one passing through a Sicilian town might readily believe that it contained the aggregate of decrepitude and deformity of Europe: the industrious inhabitants are few,—the fruges consumere nati numerous. In the square before the cathedral stands a gigantic elephant, the ancient symbol of C., with an obelisk upon his back, on the sides of which hieroglyphic characters are carved. This figure is of lava, and has withstood the concussions of many earthquakes.—C. is the see of a bishop, much of whose ample revenue is drawn from the snow sold from Ætna. There is a university of repute in this city, in which the three learned professions are taught. It has able professors; and is frequented by about 500 students; but students destined for the church far outnumber those of physic or law.—The port of C. is fit only for small craft. In 1841, 1,228 vessels = 38,068 tons, entered it. Of these 25 = 1,200 tons, were British; and <math>1,243 = 35,415</math> tons, Sicilian. The value of their cargoes was £21,450; the value of exported cargoes, £56,718. The principal exports are the snow and the fire-wood which Ætna furnishes, and which are sent to Malta and other places; various sorts of maccaroni, which are well N lat. 37° 28' 20", E long. 15° 5' 15", at the S foot of | manufactured here, and sold under the general term FREEDO NORTHE CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR of paste; silk, which is also manufactured here; sulphur, and yellow amber. - The pop. in 1831 was 52,433; in 1839, 36,000.—The ancient Catina produced Carondas the legislator, so much commended by Plato; and Stesichorus the lyric poet, who introduced singing with the accompaniment of the cithara into the chorus. The Semcetus, now the Giaratta, sung by Theocritus, flows through the plains of C. at some distance from the town. The naphtha lake, the seat of the oracle of the Palici, hardly second in repute to that of Delphos for its responses, is in the neighbourhood of C. Catina, one of the most ancient cities in Sicily, was founded in the time of the Sicanians, who were driven from it by the Siculi, at the period of their landing from Italy. From them Sicily derived the name it still retains. Having established themselves in Catina, they enacted wholesome laws, under which it prospered, until Gero I. of Syracuse, growing envious of their prosperity, and confident in his own power, attacked the city, which he sacked. He removed the inhabitants to Leontium, now Leontini, and supplied their places with new settlers from the Peloponnesus. So ambitious was Gero of the reputation of founding cities, that he attempted to abolish the name of Catina, calling that place Ætna, and himself a citizen of Ætna. His death, which happened in the first year of the 79th Olympiad, afforded a favourable opportunity, which the Sicilians eagerly availed themselves of, to drive the Grecian intruders from their territory. It next fell a prey to the Carthaginians, who proved themselves the greatest scourge it had hitherto experienced. In the 93d Olympiad, Dionysius, tyrant of Syracuse, obtained possession of Catina through the perfidy of Archilaus, the prefect of the place. The tyrant plundered the city, and sold the inhabitants for slaves. While Sicily was a province of the empire, it flourished; but on the decline of that power it fell a prey to the Saracens; and from the period of their expulsion by Roger the Norman invader, it shared the chequered fortune of the island, until its settlement by treaty upon the second branch of the royal family of Spain. C. was laid in ruins by an earthquake in 1693; and upon the site which it occupied the present city was built, the lava serving at the same time for a foundation as well as a quarry, from which stone was dug for its construction. The vallo or prov. of C. is bounded on the N and NE by that of Messina; on the E by the sea; on the S by the prov. of Syracuse; on the W by that of Calatanisetta; and on the NW by that of Palermo. It is 55 m, in length from NW to SE; and about 35 m. in greatest breadth. Its pop. in 1832 was 345,987. It is divided into 61 communes.

CATANZARO, a district and town of Naples, cap. of the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, finely situated on a hill, 5 m. from the gulf of Squillace, and 30 m. SSE of Cosenza. Pop. 13,598. It possesses some fortifications, and was formerly well-built, but has suffered much from earthquakes. Its principal buildings are the cathedral, 11 churches, the episcopal seminary, the royal academy of science, the lyceum, the foundling and two other hospitals, &c. It contains extensive manufactories of silk, velvet, cloth, &c., and has a considerable commerce in silk, grain, wine, and oil. A cattle fair is held once a-year. The women are reputed the finest in Calabria. The district of C. comprises 11 cant.
CATAPACHOS, a tribe of aborigines, in Peru, who inhabit the banks of the Pachitea, between the 9th and 10th parallels.
CATAPHRICO, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, sanjak of Janina, on the NE side of the gulf and 15 m. SE of Arta.
CATARAQUI, a river of Canada West, flowing into the St. Lawrence at Kingston, and forming the entrance to the great Rideau canal, connecting the navigation of Lake Ontario with that of the Ottawa.
CATARINA (SANTA), a town of the island and 2 m. NW of Zante, on the NE coast.

CATARROJA, a town of Spain, cap. of a judi- 1831, 14,714.—The town is 12 m. N of Saint-Quencial partido, in the prov. and 5 m. S of Valencia, on a river of the same name, which is here crossed by a handsome stone-bridge, and which falls 3 m. SE into the lake Albufera. Pop. 3,585. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a church and a custom-house. The culture of rice and fishing form the chief employments of the inhabitants.

CATAS-ALTAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 15 m. NNE of Ouro-Preto. Pop. CAT 3,000, chiefly miners. It abounds with iron. Its name is derived from the Indian word cata, signifying 'cave,' and alta, 'high.'
CATAS-ALTAS-DA-NORUEGA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 18 m. E of Queluz. Pop. 2,000, chiefly miners and agriculturists.

CATASTROPHE (CAPE), a prominent headland on the coast of S Australia, forming the W point of entrance to Spencer's gulf. Its S extremity is in S lat. 35° 1', E long. 135° 54'. It has a round smooth wooded summit.

CATAU'IXIS, a tribe of aborigines, in Brazil, in the prov. of Para, who inhabit the banks of the Madeira, Cuari, and Puru, and subsist on fish and the eggs of the tortoise, which abound in these localities.

CATAWBA, a river of the United States, which takes its rise in Burke co., North Carolina, on the E side of Blue Ridge; separates the co. of Lincoln from those of Iredell and Mecklenburg; enters South Carolina, where it takes the name of Wateree, and again, after the junction of the Congaree or Broad river, that of Santee; thence directs its course to the ESE, and, after a total course of 270 m., flows by two estuaries formed by Cedar island, into the Atlantic.
CATAWBA VIEW, a village of Caldwell co., in the state of North Carolina, U.S., on the S side of the Catawba or Santee river.

CATAWISSA, a township of Columbia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 81 m. NE of Harrisburg, at the junction of a river of the same name with the Susquehanna, and intersected by the Little Schuylkill and C. railroad. Pop. in 1840, 2,064.

CATCHINS, or CATCHINES, a Tartar tribe of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tomsk, on the banks of the Jenesei. They amount to about 6,000; and are divided into 6 small hordes. They are Shamans in religion.
CATCHBURN, or Morpeth-Castle, a township in the p. and 1½ m. S of Morpeth, in Northumberland. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 145.
CATCLIFFE, a township in the p. and 3 m. S of Rotherham, in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 930 acres. Pop. 252.
CATCOTT, a chapelry in the p. of Moorlinch, in Somersetshire, 7 m. ENE of Bridgewater. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 750.

CATEAU (LE), or CATEAU-CAMBRESIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Cambray.—The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 24,212.—The com. and town is 15 m. ESE of Cambray, on the r. bank of the Selle. Pop. in 1841, 6,880. It is a place of considerable trade and active industry. Soap, tobacco, merinos, and different linen and woollen fabrics, leather, and pottery ware, are manufactured here; and 3 large fairs are held annually. Some remains of a castle of the archbishop of C. still exist here. - In this town the treaty of peace between France, Spain, and Austria, was signed on the 3d of April, 1559.
CATEGAT. See CATTEGAT.
CATEL, or St. Mary-de-Castro, a parish in the island of Guernsey. Pop. 2,038, of whom 179 were in the v. of King's Mills.
CATELET (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Saint- Quentin.—The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in tin, on the Scheld, near its source. Pop. 610.
CATENOY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Liancourt. Pop. 630.
CATERHAM, a parish in Surrey, 3 m. NW of Godstone. Area 2,610 acres. Pop. 477.

CATERINA (Santa), a town of Naples, in Calabria-Ultra 2da, in the district and 27 m. S of Catanzaro, 3 m. from the coast. Pop. 2,000. The environs afford wine and silk. This town was much injured by the earthquake of 1783.—Also a town of Sicily, in the district and 9 m. NNW of Caltanisetta, on the Salso. Pop. 5,700.—Also a town of Sicily, in the district and 15 m. SW of Trapani, the cap. of the little island Favignana.—Also a cape in the island of Corfu, of which it forms the most northern point, in N lat. 39° 49′ 10″.—Also a canal in the prov. of Padua, in Venetian Lombardy, running from the Frassine, at a point 1 m. NW of Este, southwards to the Adige, and to the canal of Gorzon.
CATESBY-ABBEY, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. SW of Daventry. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 105.
CATFIELD, a parish in Norfolk, 12 m. NW of Norwich. Area 2,400 acres. Pop 655.
CATHARINA (SANTA), a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 6 m. SW of Alcobaça. Pop. 1,000.

CATHARINA (SANTA), a maritime province of Brazil; bounded on the N by the prov. of Sao-Paulo; on the E and SE by the Atlantic; on the SW and W by the prov. of Sao-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande. Its greatest length from N to S is 230 m. Its greatest breadth, under the parallel of 26° 30' S, is 120 m.; but its average breadth does not exceed 60 m. The river Sahi separates it on the N from the prov. of Sao-Paulo; and the Mampituba from Sao-Pedro-do- Rio-Grande, on the S, from the coast to its source in the Sierra-Catharina, which mountain-range forms the whole W boundary of the prov. Its surface has been estimated at 2,200 Portuguese sq. leagues. The principal rivers are the Avarangua, the Itajahi, and the Urussanga, which are all navigable for about 20 m. The smaller streams are the Aracari, Biguaçu, Camborice, Cubatão, Iriri-Guaçu, Mampituba, Maruki, Sahi, and Tubarao. At the N margin of the latter river is a considerable lake known as the Laguna, which is 5 leagues long from N to S, and 2 at its greatest width. The face of the country generally is mountainous. The soil is fertile, and welladapted to the culture of mandioc, Indian corn, rice, sugar, coffee, and flax.—The pop. in 1810 was estimated at 31,534; in 1841 at 67,218, of whom 12,500 were slaves. It sends a senator to the camara alta, or upper chamber, and a deputy to the general-assembly. Its provincial legislative assembly consists of 20 members.—It comprises the two large islands of Santa Catharina and San Francisco.

CATHARINA (SANTA), or St. Catherine's, an island in the S Atlantic ocean, off the coast of Brazil, between S lat. 27° 22' and 27° 50'. Its breadth from E to W is only from 2 to 5 m.; but it is about 30 m. long from N to S; and it is separated from the mainland by a channel, in some places not half-aleague in breadth. The surface of the island, and of the adjacent continent to Cape Santa-Marta-Grande, in S lat. 28° 39', is high and thickly wooded. Roussin says the island is sufficiently elevated to be seen in clear weather at the distance of 15 leagues. This island is diversified with mountains, plains, and swamps; but possesses an extremely fertile soil, and is the acknowledged garden of the Brazils. [Forster. Its produce consists in rice, maize, mandioc, coffee of excellent quality, bananas, ananas, "the finest oranges in the world" [Mawe], water-melons, pine-apples, and a variety of other fruits. Sugar and indigo are likewise produced. A profusion of beautiful flowers indicates the genial nature of the climate: myrtles appear in all parts, and the passionflower is found in equal abundance. The fern tribe are numerous and beautiful. The lands capable of cultivation have undergone considerable improvement. Extensive tracts were formerly covered with large trees, but as great quantities of wood have been cut down and used for ship-building, good timber may CAT swampy land in the island, over which the roads are carried on causeways supported by piles. These lands on account of their humidity are very favourable to the growth of rice. On the E side of the island are 3 lakes, one of which is 2 leagues in length. The surrounding seas produce a variety of excellent fish, and fine prawns. Pigs, turkeys, ducks, poultry, eggs, vegetables, and in particular excellent potatoes, are plentiful and cheap; and jerked beef and bacon are largely exported. The animal tribes are chiefly oppossums, monkeys, armadillos, and various species of serpents, among which is the beautiful coral-snake, and another very dangerous species known by the local name of the Jararaca. Of birds, there are cranes, hawks, parrots of various species, humming-birds, and toucans of every variety. The butterfly tribe are numerous. The bird-spider, Aranea avicularis, is common; and the sand-flea, Pulex penetrans. The climate is described by some travellers as serene and wholesome; the solstitial heats being moderated by fine breezes from the SW and NE, which are the winds that generally blow here. The latter prevails from September to March, and the former from April to August; so that a voyage to the N during one-half of the year is slow and tedious. Although Lesson reports that the climate of this island is unhealthy, from the prevalence of heat and moisture, yet Rear-Admiral Grenfell, who is well acquainted with it, asserts that this charge is unfounded.—The island is divided into four parishes; and is estimated to contain about 12,000 inhabitants. The male part of the pop. are chiefly engaged in cultivating the soil or in fishing; while the females make cotton cloth. It is defended by five fortresses, of which the most considerable is Santa Cruz upon the little islet Atomeri or Anhatomirim in S lat. 27° 25′ 32".—A period of 120 years elapsed from the time that Brazil was first distributed into captaincies before any colonists were established on this island, which then bore the name of Ilha-dos-Patos. In 1712, it was still covered with evergreen woods, except in the little bays and creeks opposite the mainland, where a few spots had been cleared.

CATHARINA (SANTA), or Nossa-Senhora-do- Desterro, the principal town of the above island, situated on its W shore. Its port, which is guarded by the fort of Santa Cruz, may be entered by ships of 300 tons burden. The town consists of several streets, and contains from 5,000 to 6,000 inhabitants, including slaves, and French and American residents. The houses are well-built, being generally two or three stories high, with wooden floors; and nearly all are provided with neat gardens well-stocked with vegetables and flowers. Besides the church which gives name to the town, it contains two chapels, a convent, an hospital, and good barracks. The trade of the place is inconsiderable, as the produce does not much exceed the consumption of the inhabitants. A coarse linen is made here; and the red clay which the island affords is manufactured into jars and water-pots, which are largely exported to Rio Janeiro and the Plata river. The town affords an agreeable retirement to merchants who have discontinued business; and is enlivened by the numerous coastingvessels from Bahia, Pernambuco, and other ports, bound for the Plata, which frequently touch here. It has also of late become an important place of call for ships going round Cape Horn to California.- La Perouse. — Mawe. — Roussin. — Luccock. — Langsdorf .- Foster.

CATHARINA (SANTA), a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 23 m. NE of Campanha, near the rivers Verde and Funil. The district has a pop. of 5,000, who are employed chiefly now be considered scarce. There is much low in cultivating the soil and rearing cattle. - Also a river in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, watering the districts of Cantagallo and Macahé. In 1843, an extensive plantation of mulberry-trees was formed near this river, with the view of introducing the growth of silk into the prov.

CATHARINE (MOUNT SAINT), or JEBEL KATHERINE, a lofty bi-forked mountain of Arabia, in the peninsula of Mount Sinai, under the parallel of 28° 30' N, and lying a little to the W of S from Jebel Musa or Sinai Proper. It is of granitic formation, but somewhat more rounded in its general form than Jebel Musa, which it overtops. Its "two conical summits rest on a considerable platform," as seen from the summit of Jebel Musa. [Wilson.] Its E side is clothed with rich pasturage; and a luxuriant vegetation reaches nearly to its summit [Burckhardt]; but all the rest of the landscape, as seen from Sinai, "is a sea of desolation." [Henniker.] In the narrow valley by which it is separated from Sinai is a deserted convent called El Erbayin, or 'the Convent of the Forty martyrs.' This valley is called El Ledja, and at 40 minutes' walk to the NE from the convent opens into a broader valley running SE. Ehrenberg estimates the height of the convent of Mount Sinai, in N lat. 28° 32′ 54″, at 5,400 Parisian ft. above sea-level; that of Sinai above the convent, at 2,000 ft.; and that of St. C. at 1,000 ft. more; but this is certainly erroneous, for no other travellers represent the difference in alt. between these two mountains as amounting to much more than a clearly distinguishable superiority in the alt. of St. C. Mr. Catherwood's estimate is probably much nearer the truth. He estimates the height of Sinai above the convent at about 2,000 ft.; and supposes St. C. to be from 50 to 100 ft. higher; while the elevation of the convent above sea-level he does not think can exceed 2,000 ft. Burckhardt-whose opinions, though always worthy of respect, do not command our assent in the present instance—expresses his conviction "that the Israelites encamped in the Upper Sinai, and that either Djebel Mousa or the Mount St. C. is the real Horeb." See SINAL.
CATHARINES, a township in Chemung co., in the state of New York, 16 m. N of Elmira, on Cayata creek. Pop. 2,424.

CATHCART, a parish partly in the co. of Lanark, partly in that of Renfrew, intersected by the White Cart river. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 2,349. It contains the villages of New and Old C., Langside, Clarkston, Crosshill, Crossmyloof, and Hangingshaw.
CATHEDINE, a parish of Breconshire, 7 m. NW of Crickhowell. Pop. in 1841, 175.

CATHEDRAL (MOUNT), a conspicuous summit in the mountains of Western Patagonia, on the N shore of the gulf of Trinidad. It resembles the roof and spire of a church; and is visible at sea for more than 20 leagues.
CATHERINA. See CATHARINA.
CATHERINA (SANTA), a town of Tyrol, in the district of Sondrio or Valteline, 8 m. SE of Bormio, on the N bank of the Fredolfo, in the Val Furva.
CATHERINE (CAPE SAINT), a headland of South Guinea, in the district and at the mouth of the Camma, in S lat. 1° 51'.

CATHERINE (POINT), the NE extremity of of New York, U.S., 10 m. N of Elmeira, and 190 m. Tierra-del-Fuego. It is a low, shingly point; and | WSW of Albany. Its surface is hilly, and is drained the shore continues low to Cape Espiritu Santo.
CATHERINE-ISLES, several small islets off the extreme S point of the peninsula of Boothia Felix, in N lat. 69° 20′ 37", W long. 94° 31′ 55".—Ross.
CATHERINE (SAINT), a small island of the Grecian archipelago, near the S extremity of the island of Rhodes, in N lat. 35° 52'. It is the ancient Cordylusa.

CATHERINE (SAINT), an island of the United CAT States, off the coast of the co. of Liberty, in the state of Georgia, at the mouth of the Medway and Newport, and the S of the sound of the same name. It is 11 m. in length and 6 in breadth.
CATHERINE (SAINT), a parish of Gloucestershire. Area 200 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,615.—Also a parish of Somersetshire, 4 m. NNE of Bath. Area 750 acres. Pop. 159.
CATHERINE'S ISLANDS, a small group, in the Gilolo passage, in the Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 0° 40′, E long. 129° 10′.
CATHERINGTON, a parish of Southamptonshire, 6½ m. SSW of Peterfield. Area 3,540 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,003.
CATHERSTON-LEWSTON, a parish of Dorset, 4 m. NE of Lyme-Regis, to the E of the Char. Area 90 acres. Pop. in 1841, 36.

CATHEUX, a village of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Crevecœur, 26 m. NW of Clermont. Pop. 338. It possesses some manufactories of serge and stockings. CATHILAS, CATHARGOED, and GLYNN, hamlets of Carmarthenshire, in the p. of Llanfihangel-Aberbythych. Pop. in 1841, 466.
CATHLATSKO, a river of the Oregon territory, U. S., which unites with the Columbia on the r. bank, 130 m. above the entrance of that river into the Pacific.
CATHRINEBURG, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Sieland, and 13 m. W of Copenhagen, on an affluent of the Roeskilde-fiord.

CATI, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 36 m. N of Castellon-de-la-Plana, partido of Albocacer. Pop. 1,660. It contains a church, an hospital, and several convents; and has extensive manufactories of ribbon.
CATICE, a town of SE Africa, in the territory of Malenda, on the l. bank of the Quilimancy, 140 m. WNW of Port Malenda.
CATIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, in the dist. of Citta-San-Angelo, and 9 m. S of Civita-di-Penne. Pop. 1,329. It has an annual fair.
CATIMBAO, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, parish of Rio-Bonito. It forms a ramification of the Cordilheira-dos-Aimores; and gives rise to the Chegado, an affluent of the Tingua.

CATINGA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Paracatu, which runs NE, and after a course of 75 m., of which 25 only are navigable for canoes, falls into the Paracatu near Santa-Anna-dos-Alegres. Its banks are generally thickly studded with settlements.
CATLAW, one of the Grampian mountains, in the p. of Kingoldrum, in Angus. Alt. above sealevel, 2,264 ft.
CATLENBURG, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Grubenhagen, on the l. bank of the Oder, 15 m. NNE of Gottenburg. Pop. 2,250.
CATLETTSBURG, a small village of Greenup co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., on the Ohio, below the confluence of the Big Sandy river, and 150 m. ENE of Frankfort.
CATLIN, a township of Chemung co., in the state by Catharine's creek and by tributaries of Chemung river. The soil consists of gravelly loam. Pop. 1,119.
CATLLAR, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and partido of Tarragona, near the r. bank of the Gaya.
CATMANDU. See KHATMANDU. CATMORE. A parish of Berks, 31 m. WSW of Ilsley. Area 680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 96.
CATO, a township of Cayuga co., in the state of New York, 13 m. N of Auburn. Pop. 2,380. The surface is hilly, and is watered by Seneca river in the S. The soil consists of a fertile sandy loam.
CATO-CASTRON, a town of the Grecian archipelago, in the island of Andro, on the SW coast.

CATOCHE (CAPE), a low headland of Central America, at the NE extremity of the peninsula of Yucatan, in N lat. 21° 35½, W long. 87° 6'.—The name is also given to the N point of the island of Contoy, in N lat. 21° 32', W long. 86° 48 1'.
CATOCHI, a town of Greece, in the dep. of Acarnania, on the r. bank of the Aspropotamos, 10 m. NE of its entrance into the Ionian sea, and 12 m. WNW of Missolonghi.
CATOLE, a town and parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, 5 m. SSE of Pianco. Its inhabitants are chiefly agriculturists.
CATOLE-DE-BAIXO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, parish of Catole, near the Caratheus or Poti river.
CATON, a chapelry in the township of C. and Littledale, Lancashire, 5 m. NE of Lancaster, on the Lune. Pop. in 1841, 1,195. Area of united township 8,070 acres. Pop. 1,310.
CATON, a township of Steuben co., in the state of New York, U. S., 25 m. SE of Bath. It is hilly, and is watered by tributaries of the Chemung. The soil consists of gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 797.

CATONA (LA), a village of Naples, the ancient Columna Rhegina, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 5 m. N of Reggio, on a hill near the shore of the strait of Messina. Pop. 1,800. It was destroyed by an earthquake in 1783. The naval combat in which the Duke de Vivonne defeated the Spaniards in 1675 took place in this vicinity.
CATONIO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the mountain in which the Curmatahi and Jequitahi take their rise, and on the road to Barra-do-Rio-das-Velhas.
CATORAVENIA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, in the sanj. and 18 m. NNW of Janina.

CATO'RCE, a town of Mexico, in the intendancy and 125 m. N of San-Luis-de-Potosi. It lies in a sort of hollow in the bleak cordillera of C. at an alt. of 7,760 ft. above sea-level [Wylde], or 292 ft. higher than the city of Mexico. The cordillera rises precipitately beyond and above it. The mine of La Purisima is 390 ft. higher than the town; and the Cerrode-la-Leona, 1,110 ft. The site of the town is intersected by vast ravines, which occasion no little irregularity in the buildings. It is approached by a narrow rocky path from the cassada or gorge beneath; but is abundantly supplied with provisions from the Tierra Caliente to the E, as well as from the Tableland .- Nothing can be more bleak and dreary than the appearance of the whole cordillera of C. A few narrow mule-paths, or the white bed of a torrent, alone break the monotonous colouring of the mass: yet seventy years ago, the whole district was covered with rich forests which the improvident and wasteful spirit of the miners has destroyed. The district contains extensive silver-mines which were formerly the richest in Mexico; and of which a detailed account is given in the 2d vol. of Ward's Mexico.
CATRAL, a town of Spain, in Valencia, prov. and 22 m. SE of Alicante, partido of Dolores. Pop. 2,268. It contains a church, an hospital, and a custom-house.
CATRINE, a thriving spinning village in the p. of Sorn, in Ayrshire, 2 m. E by S of Mauchline. Pop. in 1841, 2,659.
CATSCOAGWEWAY, a lake of British North America, in the Rupert's river district, connected on the S with Mistassinnie lake. CAT
CATSFIELD, a parish of Sussex, 21 m. SW of Battle. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. in 1841, 589.

CATSKILL, a township in Greene co., in the state of New York, U. S., 34 m. S of Albany. The surface is hilly towards the W, but level towards the Hudson, which washes its E border. It is watered by the C. creek and its tributaries. Pop. 5,339.—The v. of C. is pleasantly situated on both sides of the C. near its junction with the Hudson. It is an active bustling place, with several lumber-yards, tanneries, flourmills, and saw-mills. A ferry crosses the Hudson at the mouth of the C.; and the C. and Canajoharie railroad, 26 m. in length, commences here.—'The mountain-house,' as it is called, is a large hotel, perched on a table-rock in the SE part of the township, 12 m. from the v., on the brow of the C. mountains, at an alt. of 2,212 ft. above the river; and, commanding a very extensive and beautiful view of the Hudson river, and the country to the E, as far as the Green mountain range, forms a favourite summer resort. In the immediate vicinity are the Kauterskill falls, 180 ft. high.

CATSKILL MOUNTAINS, three ranges in the state of New York, belonging to the Appalachian system, which rise in Ulster co., and extending E and W through Greene and Schoharie counties, and along the Hudson river, sweep round towards the Mohawk river. On their SE flanks these mountains present a rapid descent; but on the NW, the declination is comparatively gentle. Round-Top, the highest peak, and in the most northern range, has an alt. of 3,804 ft. above tide-water; another peak rises 3,718 ft.; Mount C. Proper, which forms the southern and longest range, attains an elevation of 3,486 ft. The Susquehanna rises, in its E headstream, on the northern spine of these mountains.
CATTAL, a township in the p. of Hunsingore, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. ESE of Knaresborough, on the Nidd. Area 950 acres. Pop. in 1841, 193.

CATTARAUGUS, a county of the state of New York, U. S., comprising a superficies of 1,232 sq. m. It is generally hilly, and is watered in the N by a creek of the same name and its tributaries which flow into Lake Erie, and in the S by the Alleghany and its branches. It is also intersected by the Genesee valley canal. The soil is fertile, and affords excellent pasturage. Peat, marl, bog-iron-ore, manganese, sulphur and saline springs are found in various parts of this county. Cattle and lumber form the chief branches of local trade. Pop. in 1830, 16,726; in 1840, 28,872. Cap. Ellicottville.

CATTARO, or Bocca-di-C., the Nieder-Dal- MATIEN, OF OSTERREICHSSCHE-ALBANIEN of the Germans, and Albanie-Autrichienne of the French, a circle or administrative subdivision of the Austrian states, forming the most southern of the three portions of the Dalmatian territory, and lying around the gulf of C., the Sinus Risonicus of the ancients. Its area is estimated at 244 sq. m. The pop. in 1817 was 29,899; in 1837, 33,801. It contains the 3 towns of C., Castelnuovo, and Risano; the two market-towns of Budua and Perasto; and 116 hamlets. The productive surface was returned in 1837 at 30,622 jochs in arable ground, vineyards, and meadows; and 12,634 jochs of forest-ground. The pop. of C. are of Slavonian blood; and professing the Greek religion, have always manifested a strong leaning to Russia. They formerly constituted an independent republic; and the 7th article of the treaty by which they voluntarily submitted to the protection of the republic of Venice, in the beginning of the 15th cent., expressly states that "should the republic be unable to defend the territory of Bocca-di-C., its inhabitants shall have the right to become again independent." On this article they took their stand in refusing to acknowledge the authority of Austria when their territory was ceded to that power. In 1806 they maintained a long and resolute resistance against the attempts of the French to establish themselves.
